coredata.dc:title: Novel hybrid thermal management for Li-ion batteries with nanofluid cooling in the presence of alternating magnetic field: An experimental study
coredata.dc:creator: Kiani, Mehrdad
Omiddezyani, Soheil
Nejad, Alireza Mahdavi
Ashjaee, Mehdi
Houshfar, Ehsan
coredata.dc:description: Lithium-ion batteries' dependability, efficiency, and security significantly rely on thermal management systems. This paper aims to design a novel battery thermal management system consisting of metal foam-paraffin PCM composite, nanofluid cooling, and heat sink, which all were subjected to a magnetic field. The battery surrogate was utilized to experimentally simulate the battery heat generation, where the occurrence of the thermal runaway was considered as the most crucial criterion for battery performance. The combined effect of various conditions on the paraffin wax melting process and maximum battery temperature was also investigated. Furthermore, the battery's temperature uniformity was also measured in the presence and absence of porous media. Compared to the base case, the maximum temperature of the battery in the best scenario was reduced by about 7.5 °C, while the working time of the system is increased by 179% at Re = 1250. Likewise, this improvement on battery cooling was 151% when the Reynolds number was 890. Further, the temperature difference of the proposed hybrid battery thermal management system (BTMS) was mainly below 4 °C. The experimental results revealed that the proposed hybrid BTMS has a striking cooling effect on eliminating the generated battery's heat and transferring it to the surrounding environment.

head.ce:title: Novel hybrid thermal management for Li-ion batteries with nanofluid cooling in the presence of alternating magnetic field: An experimental study

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium-ion batteries' dependability, efficiency, and security significantly rely on thermal management systems. This paper aims to design a novel battery thermal management system consisting of metal foam-paraffin PCM composite, nanofluid cooling, and heat sink, which all were subjected to a magnetic field. The battery surrogate was utilized to experimentally simulate the battery heat generation, where the occurrence of the thermal runaway was considered as the most crucial criterion for battery performance. The combined effect of various conditions on the paraffin wax melting process and maximum battery temperature was also investigated. Furthermore, the battery's temperature uniformity was also measured in the presence and absence of porous media. Compared to the base case, the maximum temperature of the battery in the best scenario was reduced by about 7.5 °C, while the working time of the system is increased by 179% at Re = 1250. Likewise, this improvement on battery cooling was 151% when the Reynolds number was 890. Further, the temperature difference of the proposed hybrid battery thermal management system (BTMS) was mainly below 4 °C. The experimental results revealed that the proposed hybrid BTMS has a striking cooling effect on eliminating the generated battery's heat and transferring it to the surrounding environment.

ce:section.ce:para: The continuous carbon dioxide emissions by fossil fuel-based vehicles have caused severe environmental issues through the greenhouse effect. Reducing these emissions has become a challenge, and many researchers have conducted studies on the development of electric vehicles (EVs) and hybrid-electric vehicles (HEVs). These environmental-friendly vehicles can play a significant role in tackling climate change and overcoming the growing consumption rate and costs of fossil fuels. Although various types of batteries are already available in the market, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have attracted more attention since they typically have high specific energy, a long-life period, high energy density, and no memory effect. LIB, therefore, is a perfect choice for EVs and HEVs at various power ranges [1].

Meanwhile, the cell temperature in a Li-ion battery should be carefully regulated and held below a specific limit. The operation temperature has a remarkable impact on the performance of the battery. Therefore, it is crucial to keep the LIB's operating temperature below 60 °C and the temperature difference between the cells at approximately 5 °C [2]. Hence, the use of a battery thermal management system (BTMS) is necessary for regulating the maximum temperature of the battery in a desired range and dissipation of the internally generated heat by the battery module, especially in the charge and discharge process, which is generated as a result of internal resistance and battery's inner reactions [3,4].

To this date, many studies have been conducted on thermal management applications for battery modules, which are mainly divided into three general categories: passive method, active method, and hybrid thermal management. In active methods, namely air cooling [5] and liquid cooling [6], the BTMS employs direct energy for heat dissipation. In contrast, thermal management in passive methods is carried out without consuming any external power. Phase change material (PCM) [7] and heat pipe [7] are commonly applied in this method. On the other hand, hybrid thermal management combines passive and active cooling techniques [8,9]. For instance, Afzal et al. [10] thermally modeled LIB system used in EVs using finite volume techniques with the adoption of realistic coupled heat and fluid flow process. However, several studies have shown that in harsh operating conditions and high temperatures, conventional air-cooled BTMS is not sufficient to achieve uniform temperature distribution on the battery's surface [11,12]. In this regard, Ni and Wang [13] designed the air duct of the battery pack to improve the temperature uniformity of the battery pack. A parametric study was numerically carried out to analyze the effect of several critical factors on battery cooling, including the gap between the battery and the plastic support, the opening size of the upper first battery modules, the angle between the top surface of the battery packs cover plate and the horizontal face. Recently, Xu et al. [14] proposed an optimization design framework to minimize the maximum temperature difference of lithium battery packs for automotive applications. He could introduce an optimized scheme where the maximum temperature difference is reduced by 7.49% compared with the initial system, and the temperature field distribution of the lithium battery pack is also more uniform.

Chen et al. [15] discussed four different cooling strategies. It has been reported that forced air cooling had the highest energy consumption compared to other methods. Also, cooling by indirect liquid-based has proven to be a better choice than direct-cooled in EV applications because of its more effective heat dissipation. Panchal et al. [16] conducted numerical and experimental studies on the temperature distribution and velocity of liquid coolant in cold plates of mini-channel. It was concluded that the cold plates showed a significant cooling performance. Wang et al. [17] performed a numerical study to optimize the thermal performance of a pouch LIB by inserting the bionic spider-web channel cold plate on both sides of the battery for high-temperature cooling. Their simulation results showed that the channel width and angle had, respectively, the largest and smallest influence on the cooling performance of a pouch LIB. Basu et al. [18] suggested a novel BTMS based on a liquid coolant for the 18,650-type batteries. In the proposed BTMS, the battery cells were connected by aluminum parts to enhance heat dissipation by conduction. It was shown that this new design could effectively regulate the battery temperature and its distribution. Mbulu et al. [19] experimentally designed a heat pipe-based BTMS where battery surrogate was sandwiched with both I-shaped and L-shaped heat pipes under high input power. The result showed that the maximum battery temperature could not exceed 55 °C even at the highest input power.

Sefidan et al. [20] also studied thermal management of similar 18,650-type Li-ion cells by submerging each cell into a chamber containing alumina (Al2O3) nanofluid, where the ambient airflow was utilized to dissipate the excessive generated heat. The results revealed that the maximum temperature of the cells decreased significantly. Previous studies have shown that the use of nanofluids in combination with heatsinks (mini or microchannels) can enhance the efficiency of BTMS, which is attributed to the augmentation of the heat transfer rate (HTR) by the added nanoparticles [21,22]. Liu et al. [23] investigated how additive Al2O3 nanoparticles can affect the cooling performance of high-level Li-ion batteries. They found that nanoparticles can gradually reduce the maximum cell temperature, whereas they had a negligible effect on the uniformity of temperature distribution. Albeit, the impact of the same nanoparticle (i.e., Al2O3) was observed elsewhere [24], which showed acceptable performance in controlling the maximum battery temperature and surface heat distribution. Recently, Wiriyasart et al. [25] conducted a computational approach to characterize the pressure drop and temperature distribution utilizing nanofluids flowing in the corrugated mini-channel of the 18,650 type EV battery cooling module where the EV battery modules consist of 444 cylindrical Li-ion cell batteries. According to their results, temperature distributions are the most sensitive to the coolant types, flow mass flow rate, and even coolant flow direction of coolant. In another effort, the TiO2 nanofluid and Fe3O4 ferrofluid coolants were tested inside the battery pack at two different concentrations [26]. The results showed while both nanofluid and ferrofluid coolants significantly increased the pressure drop, ferrofluid coolant performs better than its nanofluid counterpart.

A novel PCM-based BTMS was proposed for the first time by Al-Hallaj and Selman [27] for EV's batteries. The most important advantage of this PCM-cooled system was the absorption of the generated heat as latent heat in the PCM. In an experimental investigation, Duan and Naterer [28] used electric heaters to emulate the heat generation inside the battery and placed the PCM in contact with the battery in two designs. Both configurations had performed effectively in maintaining the heater temperature in the desired temperature range. Putra et al. [29] studied a passive cooling system that was constructed with a heat pipe and PCM where the effectiveness of the cooling system was determined, and then the optimal PCM (Rubitherm RT 44 HC or beeswax) for a temperature range of 25–55 °C was identified.

Paraffin wax is the most widely used PCM for thermal management applications. This PCM offers several features, including high latent heat, low density, chemical stability, safety, and non-toxicity compared to the other components in a battery. Additionally, it is a cost-effective material available in abundance in nature without any corrosion effect. However, paraffin wax's low thermal conductivity, i.e., 0.4 W m−1 K−1, is a fatal disadvantage. Recently, innovative approaches have been suggested to address this issue. Suspending high thermally conductive fillers [30,31], paraffin wax embedded in a porous medium [32,33], and fins [34] are among the most common solutions.

Karimi et al. [35] conducted a numerical study on heat generation in a cylindrical Li-ion battery. Nanoparticles and metal matrices were dispersed into the paraffin PCM to increase the HTR. Three different nanoparticles, including Cu, Ag, and Fe3O4, were applied to show that the metal-PCM composite could reduce the battery bulk temperature and enhance temperature distribution. Carbon-based nanoparticles have shown great potential for thermal applications [36] due to their high thermal conductivity [37]. Also, Goli et al. [38] observed a 200-fold increment (45 vs. 0.25 W m−1 K−1) in paraffin wax's thermal conductivity by using graphene fillers embedded in pure paraffin.

Recently, hybrid porous media (metal foam-paraffin) has drawn the attention of researchers all around the world since their net-like structure enables them to increase thermal conductivity and enhance mechanical and thermophysical properties [39]. Li et al. [40] compared the cooling effect of a copper foam-paraffin composite to the BTMSs based on air-cooled and pure paraffin. Results showed that BTMS based on CF/PCM decreased the maximum battery temperature during the relatively high discharge rate, which in turn increased the effective lifespan of the Li-ion battery compared to the other two cases.

Moreover, aluminum foam can also be integrated into the paraffin to form a composite with a higher thermal conductivity than pure paraffin (0.29 W m−1 K−1–46.12 W m−1 K−1). Weng et al. [41] used aluminum foam/paraffin composite and observed that it could speed up the melting process. Hussain et al. [42] carried out an experimental study on the use of PCM-Nickel foam. The results showed that battery surface temperature was reduced to 31% and 24% compared to the coolant air and pure paraffin cases. Sun et al. [43] proposed novel structures with longitudinal fins and cylindrical rings and studied their effect on the BTMS performance. Weng et al. [44] showed that longitudinal fins perform superior to circular fins in dissipating the battery's heat. Moreover, the optimized PCM module, which used various fins, led to a 5.5% lower maximum temperature than that of the rectangular-finned module.

Mehrabi-Kermani et al. [45] introduced a hybrid BTMS consisting of two parts: air-cooling and copper metal foam saturated with paraffin. In another study, Ling et al. [46] designed a hybrid BTMS including paraffin/EG composite PCMs combined with air convection as a coolant. However, insufficient air displacement would result in heat escalation in PCMs, which eventually caused the failure of the BTMS. The proposed hybrid system successfully prevented heat accumulation in the PCM and kept the maximum temperature of the battery below 50 °C in all cycles. Behi et al. [48] proposed an integrated BTMS including PCM and heat pipe for a 18,650 battery module and observed that temperature uniformity and the maximum temperature of the battery module had improved by 73.4% and 42.7%, respectively. In recent years, applying an external magnetic field has been considered as an active technique to intensify the rate of heat transfer in double pipe mini heat exchangers [49], parallel plate channels [50], straight tubes [51], and heat sinks [52]. Meanwhile, prior works have shown that the thermal conductivity of nanofluids can be enhanced by applying an appropriate external magnetic field which creates various linear chain-like structures of nanoparticles [53]. The findings of previous research support the particle agglomeration and structure theory for thermal conductivity potency, and hence the heat transfer coefficient (
h ∝ k /
δ t
) is increased. In addition, it creates many possibilities for the use of nanofluids in various cooling applications, particularly in BTMS.

Various heat transfer enhancement techniques and designs, which utilize either constant or alternating magnetic fields, have been reported in the available literature [54–57]. Aminfar et al. [58] simulated a two-phase mixture of Fe3O4-water ferrofluid and reported an increment in the heat transfer coefficient under a magnetic field. Shahsavar et al. [59] examined the effect of magnetic fields on the heat transfer characteristics of a hybrid ferrofluid. Results showed that an external magnetic field could align the magnetic particles along the field direction, enhancing the local heat transfer coefficient. Another experimental study was conducted by Goharkhah et al. [60], where the heat transfer coefficient was increased by up to 37.3% by applying a magnetic field. Also, Omiddezyani et al. [61] experimentally studied the heat transfer characteristic of CoFe2O4/rGO-water nanofluid subjected to a magnetic field and observed a 31.1% enhancement of Nusselt number.

In light of the prior studies and following our recently published works [62–64], the present experimental research intends to investigate is to the effect of the presence of an external magnetic field on battery cooling. In this regard, a novel BTMS is proposed based on the integration of nanofluid, phase change material, porous media, and aluminum heat sink, which is eventually exposed to the an alternating magnetic field. This experimental study is performed in a Li-ion battery surrogate in the presence of an external alternating magnetic field., To the best of authors's knowledge there is no previous study investigationg the effect of a magnetic field on a hybrid BTMS,. Two nanofluids, CuO and Fe3O4, were used to emphasize the impact of Ferro-nanofluid Fe3O4-water slurry and its magnetic response to an external magnetic field. Since the experiments were done under harsh thermal conditions, i.e., high temperature and thermal runaway, the battery cell was substituted with an electrical film heater. Therefore, the PCM saturated with porous copper foam and nanofluids with high thermal conductivity were integrated, and a magnetic field was applied for cooling the battery module. Nanofluids were utilized as liquid coolants in this research, and their effect on BTMS was studied in terms of nanoparticle volume fraction, Reynolds number, heat flux, and magnetic field intensity. The proposed integrated system, which includes both active and passive techniques, is expected to perform as a highly viable and effective option for cooling applications.

Employing PCM in the battery pack design is one of the effective passive thermal management techniques. The PCM used in the present work was commercial paraffin (P 42–44 #107150) utilized in its block shape, which is water-insoluble, non-toxic, odorless, and chemically stable. The PCM was supplied from MERCK Co., whose melting temperature range depends on its type. The KS-1 probe made of stainless steel (KD2-Pro, USA) was used for effective thermal conductivity measurement. The temperature of the solid and liquid phases was set at 25 ± 0.5 °C and 45 ± 0.5 °C using a constant thermal bath (FP40-HC, JULABO GmbH Co., Germany), respectively. Hence, the thermal conductivity of paraffin at the temperatures mentioned above was experimentally obtained equal to 0.317 and 0.205 W m−1 K−1 (measuring error: 0.05), respectively. The paraffin density was determined as 700 (at 600 °C) and 900 (at 20 °C) kg m−3 in the liquid and solid phase. The thermophysical properties of the paraffin used are tabulated in Table 1 . Thermo-physical properties of water and nanoparticles are presented in Table 2 .

A metal foam made from copper was used in this study as a highly conductive frame to facilitate phase change of paraffin, originating from its net-like structure. Owing to its high thermal conductivity, copper foam was preferred over the other two commonly used materials (Cu and Al). The size of the copper foam was 130 mm × 80 mm × 32 mm with porosity of 0.9 (ω = 20 and ε = 0.9) and PPI (pore per inch) of 20, (Fig. 1 -(a)). To prevent escape bubbles or splits, the copper foam was prepared in a container of the same size filled with paraffin.

The procedure of melting paraffin injection into the foam is highly sensitive. It must be done accurately as the maximum paraffin temperature must not exceed 80 °C according to the information provided by the manufacturer. The setting of injection temperature, therefore, was set equal to 70 °C. It is noteworthy that the copper foam-PCM composite was vibrated by a mechanical vibrator for 1.5 h when the molten paraffin was being poured into the metal foam to create a uniform blend of copper foam-paraffin.

Another common approach for passive BTMS is to employ a heat sink. Heat sinks are typically fabricated from copper, aluminum, or other materials with a high thermal conduction level. Herein, a special kind of aluminum heat sink (Finned and Smooth) with a pin-fin height of 2 mm throughout the channel was studied for its application in the BTMS (Fig. 1- (c & d)). The nanofluid flowed through the pneumatic hoses into the canal (13 cm
×
7 cm), and after the collision of nanoparticles with the heat sink wall, they moved towards the test section outlet. The thickness and height of each fin were 3 mm and 2 mm, respectively. The aim was to make the fluid flow more turbulent, thereby enhancing the HTR. Meanwhile, besides using silicone insulators, the canal was sealed correctly by using screws. Moreover, the outer surface of the test apparatus consisted of translucent 2 mm thick Plexiglas sheets, which offer unique characteristics such as transparency (observation of the paraffin melting process), heat resistance, and low coefficient of thermal conductivity.

The test apparatus was divided into two symmetrical parts that each part constituted the Mica heater, copper foam, insulators, and steel plate. Additionally, a copper foam was implanted on each side, and the fluid passed through the center of the two parts (Fig. 1-(b)).

The experimental setup included heat sink, steel plates, heater, pressure drop measurer (Endress-Hauser, UK), data acquisition system, metal foam-paraffin composite, and eight K-type thermocouples (Testo Co., Germany, 0.5% uncertainty). A Variac transformer was also included in the setup, which supplied the applied voltage in the battery surrogate and imposed a steady heat flux on the surface of the steel plate. By adjusting the pump, the fluid passed through the copper coils, submerged in the thermal bath. The fluid then reached the flowmeter and moved towards the end of the canal. Herein, the turbulence level decreased by passing through the flow-parallelizer. In the following, the fluid left the channel after collision with the nine fins.

Moreover, eight thermocouples were mounted on the different parts of the system. Two of them monitored the bulk temperature of the nanofluid at the canal's inlet and outlet, while the others recorded the heater temperature. Also, the non-conductive mica was not ideal for fastening thermocouples. They were bound to the ultrathin copper sheet (THK:0.25 mm), which was stuck to the heater, owing to copper's high thermal conductivity and compatibility with thermocouple wires. Eventually, all data was received during the test by the data logger and was returned to the machine for further analysis.

A signal generator, a DC power supply, and electromagnets were the three main components of the magnetic sub-circuit used in this work. Three U-shaped electromagnets are used to generate a magnetic field, each having two copper windings placed into its center. Every U-shaped electromagnetic winding was wrapped with 3000 turns of 0.5 mm diameter copper wire and had the equivalent electric resistance of 35 Ω. The cores were made from iron-silicon alloy powder and had an insulating layer with relatively low hysteresis and high saturation magnetic flux, which eventually reduced the energy loss. Fig. 2 -(b and c) shows a schematic view and components of the electromagnets.

The direct current generated by the DC power supply (0–60 V) was transmitted to an alternating current using a signal generator. It is worth mentioning that a microcontroller was carefully inserted into the test circuit to adjust the pulse frequency and the phase shift of an alternative magnetic field. Fig. 3 schematically illustrates the entire course of the experiment setup where the magnet sub-circuit is featured. Also, various configurations of experiments with water and nanofluid are presented in Fig. 4. Magnetic field intensity has been measured accurately through experiments using a Teslameter. HT201 Teslameter used in the present tests accounted for a maximum magnetic field flux of 600 G where an inlet electric current of 1.7 was prescribed to the magnetic sub-circuit. AC frequency is as follows:

Where

is the disconnection and connection time (both are equal) of an alternating magnetic field.

Heat generation was cell-based mainly on two essential factors, namely reversible and irreversible heat. Irreversible heat was due to over-potential and electrical resistance, whereas reversible heat originated from entropy changes. Meanwhile, Bernardi et al. [65] suggested the following equation to describe the rate of the generated heat due to the electrochemical interactions:
(2)

(

)
− I

T
(
d E

)
]

I, V, T, and E denote the charge/discharge current, operating voltage, local temperature in Kelvin, and open-circuit voltage. Both V and E depend on the temperature and the SOC (state of charge). In Eq. (2), the first term (I(E-V)) implies irreversible heat loss, which is related to concentration polarization, activation polarization, and ohmic polarization. Also, the second term (I[T(dE/dT)]) signifies the reversible heat loss, which is attributed to changes in the open-circuit potentials considering temperature differences in the electrodes.

Despite various benefits of LIBs, safety concerns hinder their widespread application in large-scale utilization. These safety issues are mainly due to a phenomenon known as thermal runaway. This phenomenon is caused by the accumulation of heat due to inefficient or lack of cooling system, overcharging, and short circuit fault because of flammable organic solvents within them. Hence, using efficient battery thermal management systems is necessary to prevent the occurrence of such safety risks.

A thermal runaway is an uncontainable exothermic reaction that may result in the ignition or explosion of the battery. Thus, using original batteries for research purposes on thermal management is somewhat hazardous. Additionally, leaving batteries in harsh conditions and extreme operating temperatures will significantly increase the risk of battery degradation and reduce the battery performance, which eventually can result in thermal runaway. Thus, in this study, a battery surrogate (film heater) was used to implement harsh conditions instead of an actual battery used in the previous studies [42,45]. Because mica can prevent any electrical contact, they were utilized to make film heater. The inner surface of the film heater was covered with a 0.2 mm aluminum sheet, while its outer shell was in contact with the PCM-copper foam.

With having an AC power supply (0–3 A and 0–300 V), Ohm's law was used to calculate the thermal power as follows:
(3)
P =
V 2
R
where R denotes electrical resistance (900 Ω). Furthermore, to avoid any forms of heat loss, an elastomeric layer (h = 0.03 W m−1 K−1) was applied to isolate the heater's lateral faces. Also, the small thickness to surface ratio of the heater facilitated cooling only from the surface.

The Fe3O4 and CuO nanoparticles used in this experiment had an average particle size of 30 nm and purity of 99% and were prepared by chemical coprecipitation. The magnetic nanofluids used in the experiments were prepared with 4%, 2%, and 1% volume concentrations. Furthermore, the ultrasonic process (Hielscher Ultrasonics™ Sonotrode, Germany) broke the agglomeration of metallic nanoparticles, resulting in a stable nanofluid.

The experimental results are discussed in this section. Table 3 shows the baseline experiment, while Table 4 summarizes ten experiments conducted under various operational conditions. The experiments were designed to study the performance of BTMS in a Li-ion battery and particularly to investigate the effect of the presence of a magnetic field.

To ensure that the battery's temperature matches the melting temperature of the paraffin wax, the BTMS performance needs to be adjusted. In this study, the performance of the BTMS is evaluated until the surface temperature of the battery reaches its maximum value, or the paraffin wax melts entirely, and thermal runaway condition occurs. The cardinal objectives here are (1) extending the useful life of Li-ion battery, i.e., delaying thermal runaway condition, and (2) reducing the maximum possible battery temperature, i.e., battery discharge process, and (3) addressing the effect of AC magnetic field on the performance of BTMS. By performing numerous and accurate analyses, the reproducibility of the test results was verified. All tests were performed at T = 25 °C, i.e., ambient temperature. The fluid entered the aluminum canal, where the inlet temperature was equal to the ambient temperature. Tests were conducted using three different volume fraction values of the nanofluid, including VF-1%., VF-2%, and VF-4%, to study nanoparticle concentration in based nanofluid on the performance of the BTMS.

Additionally, to study the effect of nanofluid inlet velocity on the BTMS performance, four different values of Re numbers, including 380, 630, 890, and 1250 were selected. Three different values of the heating power of the battery surrogate were used to evaluate the efficiency of BTMS under normal and harsh conditions. The chosen values were 30 W, 58 W, and 71 W for cases in which copper foam, Al canal, and PCM were used, respectively. The temperature of the upper and lower battery cells is a well-known parameter in the thermal analysis of BMTS within various cooling systems. Therefore, the difference between the temperature of the upper and lower battery cells is as critical as the battery's maximum temperature. Despite the transient nature of the problem, the steady-state condition can be achieved by the battery cooling system in some tests, whereas the safety of the battery is still ensured.

To evaluate the impact of nanofluid type on the performance of the BTMS, both Re numbers and nanofluid volume fraction were fixed at 600 and 2%, respectively. The carrier fluid was deionized water, and the slurry contained two different metallic nanoparticles. Fig. 5 -(b) represents how the two well-known metallic nanoparticles, Fe3O4 and CuO, affect the battery's temperature. As expected, the additive nanoparticles resulted in a significant enhancement in the thermal performance of the BTMS. This increase is attributed to the heat transfer inside the battery through forced convection in the presence of nanofluid. In summary, the presence of nanofluid can delay the battery's runaway condition, allowing it to continue being in a safe state for an extended time. The enhancement of the slurry's HTC was due to metal oxide, which was uniformly distributed throughout the base fluid. This contribution in the slurry HTC directly resulted from the high thermal conductivity of metal oxide [69].

The HTC of the slurry depends on the thermal conductivity of the nanofluid and the thickness of the boundary layer. Unlike heat conductivity, the thermal boundary layer thickness is inversely correlated to the heat transfer coefficient of the slurry. Heat can be transferred at the interface of the nanoparticle and the base fluid, e.g., nanoparticle-water slurry. The considerable improvement in the battery cooling system due to the dissolution of the additive nanoparticles in the base fluid is demonstrated in Fig. 5 (b). It can be observed that the use of nanoparticles with high thermal conductivity led to a more significant decline in the maximum temperature of the battery. Therefore, as shown in Fig. 5(b), the reduction in the temperature for CuO-Water slurry was more significant compared to pure water and Fe3O4-water slurry. Fig. 5 concluds that three main factors are responsible for the heat transfer enhancement inside the battetry: (1) the Brownian motion of nanoparticles, (2) particle clustering effect, and (3) constitution of layers at the interface between the base fluid and the nanoparticles. Experimental results showed that the BTMS containing CuO-water decreased the maximum temperature of the battery by about 4 °C when the time reached 2250 s, compared to the base case. It can be related to the high thermal conductivity of copper.

As seen in Fig. 5(b), the test results follow a similar pattern for various nanoparticles as coolants. A rapid increment of the battery's maximum temperature occurred during the first 380 s. After that, the growth rate of the curves decreased. Once the battery's runaway condition became worse, an abrupt rise occurred after the plateau. As a result, the second jump in the temperature value is dependent on the nanoparticle material. The second leap occurs later if the thermal conductivity decreases, e.g., it happens for CuO and Fe3O4 after 3080 s and 2420 s, respectively. In summary, the enhancement achieved in the thermal performance of the BTMS by adding CuO and Fe3O4 nanoparticles was 43.2% and 12.6% higher than that obtained by adding pure water, respectively.

The thermal performance of BTMS in a canal with and without fins is compared in Fig. 5(c). The results revealed that using a heat sink with uniform fin height decreased the maximum temperature and delayed the complete phase change of the paraffin compared to that of a heat sink without fins. The working time of the battery increased from 3080 s to 3510 s and from 2420 s to 2830 s for copper oxide and iron oxide, respectively. To eliminate the effect of temperature on the test results, the temperature was carefully monitored for all tests to maintain a temperature of 25 °C, which is close to the entering temperature of the fluid. Experimental results indicate that the BTMS with a finned canal prolongs the working time of the battery by 31.7–63.2%, compared to the base case.

The temperature of LIBs had a considerable effect on their performance, highlighting the importance of BTMS in a LIB. It is worth mentioning that besides the temperature of the battery, the uniform distribution of the temperature inside the battery can have a substantial impact on the practical life, performance, and efficiency of the battery. Similar observations have been reported previously [70].

In this section, a relatively strong magnetic field was applied to the nanofluid to study its effect on the laminar forced convection heat transfer inside the battery. The nanofluids Fe3O4-Water and CuO-Water slurries with 2% volume fraction were used where the Reynolds number and the magnetic flux density were set equal to 630 and 600 G, respectively. The magnetic field frequency in all experiments was equivalent to 10 Hz (if applicable). Fig. 5(d) shows that the magnetic field can favorably affect the performance of BTMS. Since the magnetic nanoparticles can respond to the magnetic field, the HTR increased, consequently delayed the battery's runaway condition. Experimental results indicate that the BTMS containing Fe3O4-water nanofluid, which is exposed to a magnetic field, prolongs the working time of the battery by 50.1% and 97.5%, compared to case 2 and base case, respectively. This significant improvement inthermal performance is mainly attributed to the presence of a magnetic field wherein ferro-nanoparticles are subjected to the magnetic force along the direction of the magnetic field. Due to the angle between flow direction and the resultant force acting on nanoparticles, the nanoparticles tend to rotate while moving away from the flow direction. This vortical motion of nanoparticles in nanofluid facilitates the heat transport mechanism between carrier fluid and nanoparticles, enhancing convective heat transfer.

Furthermore, Nano-ferro-particles were attracted to the magnetic walls of the canal due to the familiar Kelvin force. Therefore, the accumulation of magnetic nanoparticles in the vicinity of the canal wall triggers an abrupt increase in the thermal conductivity of the mixture, reducing the thickness of the thermal boundary layer. Indeed, the heat conduction was influenced by the well-ordered chains parallel to the field direction and the accumulated nanoparticles in the vicinity of the wall impeds the nanofluid motion, disturbs the thermal boundary layer and hence its thickness is reduced.

To sum up, there was a substantial increment in heat transfer rate by employing a magnetic field. For example, the maximum temperature of the battery surface was 322.5 K, corresponding to the pure water coolant, which was then dropped to 318.3 K in the presence of a ferrofluid (i.e., Fe3O4-Water) under the 600 G magnetic field intensity. As expected, the magnetic field's presence did not significantly influence the non-magnetic nanofluid, i.e., CuO-Water, in Fig. 5(d).

The impact of nanofluid inlet velocity was studied in this section by performing a set of experiments on both pure water and magnetic Fe3O4-Water nanofluid. An AC magnetic field with a frequency of 10 Hz was applied to the finned canal. All tests' heat and magnetic flux values were set equal to 58 W and 600 G, respectively. The evolution of the battery temperature for four different Re numbers is demonstrated in Fig. 6 (a). Note that the coolant inlet temperature was
25
°C
in all experiments. It can be observed in Fig. 6(a) that the performance of the BTMS was enhanced with increasing Re number. According to this figure, the coolant's heat absorption was more significant at higher Re numbers due to the moderate temperature gradient corresponding to the flow at a higher Re number, e.g., Re = 1250. Therefore, increasing the Reynolds number of the coolant fluid can increase the battery's useful life. Moreover, a higher Reynolds number can also delay the thermal runaway condition in the BTMS in a Li-ion battery. For example, at a Reynolds number of 890, the thermal runaway condition occurs after 5400 s, when the temperature of the aluminum surface and copper plate, i.e., the surface of the battery, reached 317.3 K and 321.5 K, respectively.

A similar trend is observed for all curves in Fig. 6(a). During the first 200 s of the tests, solid PCM did not undergo any phase change. However, the maximum temperature of the battery suddenly increases throughout the time in all tests. Therefore, PCM did not play a significant role at this stage. After that, the phase change process initiated gradually in the paraffin wax, which consequently resulted in a drastic increase in the cooling rate of the battery. The heat absorption by the PCM continued until the PCM was completely melted. Once the PCM phase transformation was completed, it could no longer store more latent energy. Afterward, there was a jump in the maximum temperature of the battery, which caused thermal runaway conditions in the Li-ion battery. It is still possible to delay this abrupt increase in the battery's temperature, e.g., by increasing the inlet velocity (Re = 890 or 1250). Results indicate that when the Reynold number of the system has been set to 890 and 1250, the BTMS prolongs the working time of the battery by 41.1% and 27%, respectively, compared to case 3. In addition, BTMS can promote this time to 151% and 179% at mentioned Reynolds numbers compared to the base case.

As shown in Fig. 6(a), the performance of BTMS at a low Reynolds number of coolant flow (Re = 380 or 630) was not acceptable. The uniform dispersion of the metallic nanoparticles within the base fluid was responsible for the low performance of BTMS at a low Re number regime. Also, the distribution of metallic nanoparticles was not uniform throughout the aluminum canal. The tendency of nanoparticles to agglomerate on inner parts of the channel rather than the vicinity area of the wall caused nanoparticle aggregation at high Reynolds number values, which subsequently increased the HTR.

The battery's temperature for the magnetic nanofluid, i.e., Fe3O4-water, and pure water versus time under various heat flux is demonstrated in Fig. 6(b), where a 600 G magnetic field with a frequency of 10 Hz was applied. Constant values of 30 W, 58 W, and 71 W were considered for the battery heating power. The graphs in Fig. 6(b) show how the battery's temperature reached its steady-state value. A stable period for the battery was achieved by BTMS only at small amounts of heat flux, e.g., 30 W. Therefore, the role of nanofluid in the battery as a coolant was negligible in other cases as it was unable to eliminate the heat generated by the battery efficiently. As seen in Fig. 6(b), pure water was not a good option for battery coolant at the power of 30 W, while adding only 2% (by volume) of iron oxide, i.e., Fe3O4-Water slurry could guarantee the safe operating condition of Li-ion battery at the same heating power. Experimental results show that the BTMS prolongs the working time of the battery by 82.3% compared to the pure water system under the heating power of 71 W.

Although the test results indicated the positive influence of additive PCM to nanofluid slurry on the efficiency of the thermal management system, such a positive effect was limited to a specific range of thermal operating conditions of the LIB. The favorable impact on the battery cooling is wholly confined to its performance under the temperature of phase change completion. When the PCM is entirely molten, the thermal resistance between the surrounding environment and the cell increases significantly. Therefore, after completing phase change, the negative impact of nanofluid on the battery coolant process became evident. As a result, the PCM could not positively affect the cooling process of the battery over complete phase change temperature. At this stage, air as a coolant was adequate.

This section discusses how the volume fraction of magnetic nanoparticles can affect the battery's cooling efficiency. For all tests, the power and Re number for the magnetic Fe3O4-Water slurry was set equal to 58 W and 600, respectively. Three different values of volume fraction applicable to the thermal performance of a Li-ion battery were chosen. Fig. 6(c) shows the evolution of the battery's maximum temperature. As expected, an increase in the number of metallic nanoparticles reduced the maximum temperature. This outcome concurs with those of previous findings [71,72].

For example, at Reynolds number of 630, the melting process of the paraffin wax took 3630 s for 1% volume concentration. Besides, increasing the volume concentration to 2% and 4% increases the required time for melting to 4250 s and 4570 s, respectively. This thermal performance enhancement is also a result of the slip between the base fluid and the nanoparticles and thus the disruptions caused by the nanoparticles in the flow due to the more chaotic movement of the particles in the base fluid. According to Fig. 6(c), the battery life can be prolonged by increasing the volume fraction of the metallic nanoparticles. Experimental results show that the BTMS (4% volume concentration) decreased the maximum temperature of the battery by about 5.5 °C and prolonged the battery's working time by 112.5%, compared to the base case. It can be concluded that the random movement of the nanoparticles, which is known as the Brownian motion, facilitates heat transfer that ultimately postpones the battery runaway condition. This Brownian motion becomes more pronounced by increasing the Ferro-nanofluid's volume fraction, increasing the probability of particle-wall, particle-liquid, and particle-particle interactions. The cooling performance improvement in battery with volume fraction can be attributed to the micro-convection around each nanoparticle and stirring action caused by nanoparticle's Brownian motion in the carrier nanofluid. This stirring action can create a continuous momentum and energy exchange between nanoparticles and slow-moving particles versus carrier nanofluid [60].

The effective thermal conductivity of a nanofluid where Brownian motion induces micro-convection in the vicinity of the particles has been studied extensively. The chaotic motion of nanoparticles within the fluid and improved thermal conductivity enhance a two-component fluid's heat transfer coefficient [61]. However, nanoparticles dissolved in a carrier nanofluid can have a negative impact on the cooling performance of the battery as its volume fraction exceeds 5% [62]. Many nanoparticles dissolved in a heavily concentrated nanofluid resist the fluid motion, resulting in a significant increase in nanofluid viscosity. Nanoparticles generally tend to deposit because of the intermolecular interactions such as Van der Waals forces. An increment in the nanoparticles' volume fraction can increase the deposition rate of the nanoparticles within the carrier nanofluid as the particles form clusters with time. This clustering constrains the translational and rotational Brownian motion, which negatively affects the performance of the BTMS.

Additionally, the effect of the volume fraction on Ferro-nanofluids in the presence of a magnetic field should be investigated separately. In contrast to a non-magnetic slurry, the HTR is expected to increase with nanoparticle volume fraction when a strong magnetic field is applied. This phenomenon is discussed in detail in the following.

Ferro-nanofluid was exposed to a 10 Hz AC magnetic field to study its effect on battery cooling efficiency. The volume fraction and Re number for Ferro-nanofluid were set equal to 2% and 630, respectively. Fig. 6(d) demonstrates the curve parameter of the battery surface temperature plot versus magnetic field intensity. Two magnetic field intensities of 300 G and 600 G were chosen for this purpose. As shown in Fig. 6(d), applying an external magnetic field favorably impacts the thermal performance of Fe3O4 nanofluid battery system so that a stronger magnetic field can lead to more delay in the onset of a runaway condition. For instance, the required time to reach battery runaway condition increased from 3300 s to 4250 s when the intensity of the magnetic field was doubled. It can be inferred that the distribution of the laminar boundary layer which in turn is proportional to magnetic field strength accounts for improvment of battery thermal performance.

In this section, two experiments were conducted in the presence and absence of metal foam to investigate the impact of the hybrid system on the cooling process of the battery. Unlike the conventional battery system with pure paraffin, the hybrid system consisted of metal foam and paraffin. The coolant was a Fe3O4-Water slurry, and constant heating power of 58 W was used for all experiments. Fig. 7 (a) shows the evolution of the surface temperature of the battery.

Due to the large heat capacity and low thermal conductivity of paraffin wax (0.205 W m−1 K−1) can be used as a good insulator in many applications. The PCM part in the vicinity area of the battery cell melted initially due to the low HTR in paraffin wax, while the far part was still in the solid phase. The molten area acted as a heat sink, absorbing the energy required for its phase transition. However, the liquid portion of the PCM served as an insulator, which postponed the complete phase transition of the PCM. Finally, after 400 s, the maximum temperature of the battery surface exceeded 329.1 K. An alternative to conventional battery systems with paraffin wax is a hybrid system. Also, the effective thermal conductivity of metal foam-paraffin, i.e., the hybrid system, was 40 W m−1 K−1. Hence, the metal foam-paraffin system was approximately 200 times more conductive than pure paraffin and could decrease the maximum temperature by 22.5%. Besides, the metal foam leads to a uniform distribution of thermal energy in the battery owing to its cellular structure. Therefore, a hybrid system can outperform its conventional counterpart in increasing the battery's lifespan. For example, the completion time of the melting process of paraffin using a hybrid approach was approximately 3800 s longer than that of the conventional pure paraffin system, demonstrating the effectiveness of the hybrid system (Fig. 6(d)).

Fig. 7(b) shows a schematic view of the general variation trend in the maximum temperature within the LIB. The effect of the presence of PCM on the temperature of the battery is also included in this figure. The cooling process of a LIB in the attendance of PCM can be outlined in three stages as follows:

Stage I: this stage is also referred to as the O-Melting point stage. The battery's surface temperature and the nanofluid and battery's temperature difference are relatively small. The latter is the driving force for heat transfer from the battery to the coolant. Therefore, the effect of pure water coolant in cooling the battery is insignificant. As a result, the battery's highest temperature increases significantly over time. This problem can be addressed by adding nanoparticles at an appropriate volume fraction. As discussed earlier, a higher volume fraction hinders abrupt changes in the maximum temperature, and reduces the difference between the temperature of the surface and the inside of the battery.

Stage II: The latent heat is stored within paraffin wax during its phase change process. The surface temperature of the battery remains nearly unchanged when paraffin melts gradually. This constant thermal condition confirms that the BTMS, i.e., nanofluid-paraffin, can successfully remove the entire heat generated within the LIB.

Stage III: When the battery discharges completely, it undergoes an extreme temperature change, and the battery runaway condition is unavoidable. Similar to Stage I, the heat generated can be removed with nanofluids.

Fig. 7(c) confirms the melting area and the melting time of paraffin-metal foam composite in the experiment. The role of PCM in absorbing the battery heating power became evident when it started to melt. Battery heating power can be generally classified into two categories: (1) Heat absorption by the PCM in the form of its latent heat of melting and (2) Heat sink, where heat is transferred through a typical heat transfer mode of conduction. Clearly, for a BTMS without any metal-foam paraffin, the battery heating power is solely absorbed by the PCM, contributing to its phase change process. Therefore, the complete melting process terminates much earlier than the hybrid systems for a pure paraffin wax system.

As discussed earlier, the temperature difference between battery cells is a vital thermal parameter that impacts the lifespan and performance of the battery. Depending on the battery type, as the high-temperature difference value exceeds a specific range of maximum temperature difference values, it can create reliability issues. The test results of the present research recorded 22 °C for non-porous testing, whereas that of the hybrid testing was equal to 4 °C (copper foam-PCM) (Fig. 8 (a)). It can be concluded that this low-temperature difference is due to the porous structure of the metal foam, which has a favorable effect on conductive heat distribution. The maximum temperature decreased by 18.9% in copper foam, while in the absence of metal foam, thermal runaway occurred at t = 400 s. It should be noted that the “without-metal foam” test was continued until the temperature difference exceeded 22 °C since, beyond this temperature, the experiment will be associated with safety hazards.

In contrast to thermal parameters, the pressure drop is an essential hydraulic parameter that clearly describes the nanofluid motion within the aluminum canal, which directly influences the thermal performance of BTMS. A suitable pump providing consumption power is required to offset the inevitable pressure drop across the channel. The system's total cost depends on the pump's power, which increases with the pressure drop. Admittedly, high values of pressure drop adversely affect the efficiency of the battery. Fig. 8 shows the pressure drop against the Re number for different volume fractions. This figure depicts the effect of pressure drop on BTMS for both pure water and Fe3O4-Water slurry. Although additive nanoparticles can improve the thermal performance of BTMS, they can cause hydraulic deficiency. For example, as shown in Fig. 8(b), pure water outperformed all nanofluid cooling systems in terms of pressure drop. Generally, the pressure drop is directly proportional to nanoparticles' volume concentration and the Reynolds number [73]. A 55.2% increase in pressure drop is observed while the volume fraction of nanoparticles varying between 1 vol% to vol4%, corresponding to Reynolds number of 890. A larger pump was required to supply the necessary power for higher concentrated nanofluids. Although a more considerable pressure drop was observed at higher flow rates, the negative influence of adding 2% nanoparticles to the base fluid (pure water) on the pressure drop was less pronounced at higher Re numbers. However, as seen in Fig. 8(b), it did not depend on the Reynolds number when 1% nanofluid was used.

The effect of fins on pressure drop in an aluminum canal is shown in Fig. 8(c). Experimental data for pressure drop were collected at different Reynolds numbers regarding case II. The results indicated that pressure drop in a finned canal was more significant than that of the non-finned counterpart. It can be attributed to the frictional energy dissipation due to nanofluid viscosity.

This paper presents the operational analysis of LIBs using the hybrid cooling method. Two different types of nanofluids were used as the coolant, and the performance of BTMS in various operating conditions was analyzed. A symmetrical part of the battery module was considered for the experiment design. The phase change process within PCM can significantly contribute to the cooling and heating processes as the heat transfer occurs at a constant temperature. Thus, the hybrid BTMS could be a promising thermal management solution for Li-ion batteries. The conclusions can be summarized as follows:
(1)
The existence of magnetic nanoparticles suspended within the base fluid can improve thermal conductivity potency either in the presence or absence of a magnetic field and, hence bring further reducing the maximum temperature of the battery.
(2)
Adding a sufficient amount of nanoparticles to the base fluid improves the convective heat transfer coefficient significantly. Also, the performance of the battery improves as the volume fraction of nanoparticles increases.
(3)
Using nanofluids to improve heat transfer rate may impose adverse effects on the entire system, such as pressure drop, which causes extra energy consumption. Additionally, by increasing the volumetric concentration and flow rate, this issue could be pronounced due to the increased viscosity.
(4)
Integrating passive methods (PCM) with efficient active methods effectively postpones the thermal runaway condition and reduces the maximum temperature of the battery. As a result, the reliability of the thermal management system improves as well.
(5)
At Re = 1250, Fe3O4/water nanofluid as coolant of battery thermal management system can maintain the maximum temperature in the safe operating range of 318.7 K.
(6)
Despite their unique features, PCMs have some drawbacks, such as leakage inside the battery and low thermal conductivity. Therefore, the experiments are strongly recommended to be performed cautiously when PCM is used. Meanwhile, it is possible to enhance the thermal stability of BTMS by adequately adjusting the liquid channel structure and coolant fluid properties.
(7)
The rate of heat transfer promotes notably with magnetic field intensity. The strength of the magnetic field directly influences the thermal boundary layer thickness and slurry thermal conductivity.
(8)
Compared to the original BTMS (base case), the thermal runaway condition was delayed by about 3850 s using the optimized BTMS (case 4, Re = 1250).
(9)
In case 8, the maximum temperature and temperature difference decrease by 22.5% and 79.3% compared to that obtained by BTMS with pure paraffin, respectively. Therefore, the uniform distribution of the temperature is significantly improved.
